problems.problem: Explain the process of natural selection using the example of the peppered moth (Biston betularia). Include in your answer: (a) Variation in the population (2 marks) (b) Selection pressure (2 marks) (c) Inheritance of advantageous traits (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: In the peppered moth population, there was naturally occurring genetic variation in wing color. Two main forms existed: a light, speckled form (typica) and a dark, melanic form (carbonaria). This variation was controlled by a single gene with two alleles. Before the Industrial Revolution, the light form was more common because it was better camouflaged against the lichen-covered tree trunks where the moths rested during the day. The dark form was rare because it was more visible to predators (birds) against the light-colored bark. This variation provided the raw material upon which natural selection could act.

The selection pressure that acted on the peppered moth population was predation by birds. Birds hunted moths by sight, and moths that were more visible against their resting background were more likely to be eaten. During the Industrial Revolution in 19th-century England, air pollution from coal-burning factories killed lichen on tree trunks and coated them with dark soot. This changed the environment: tree trunks became darker. Suddenly, the previously well-camouflaged light moths became highly visible, while the previously rare dark moths became better camouflaged. The selection pressure (bird predation) now favored the dark morph, which survived and reproduced more successfully.

The dark coloration was genetically determined and could be passed from parents to offspring. Dark moths that survived predation were more likely to reproduce and pass the dark allele to their offspring. Over successive generations, the frequency of the dark allele increased in the population, while the frequency of the light allele decreased. By the late 19th century, the dark form made up over 90% of the peppered moth population in industrial regions of Britain. This demonstrated natural selection in action: a heritable trait that conferred a survival advantage increased in frequency over time.

problems.final_answer: (a) Variation: Light and dark forms existed due to genetic differences. (b) Selection pressure: Bird predation; in polluted areas, dark moths were better camouflaged against soot-darkened trees and survived more. (c) Inheritance: Dark moths passed the dark allele to offspring, increasing its frequency over generations.

problems.problem: (a) State two observations Darwin made during his voyage on the HMS Beagle. (2 marks) (b) Explain how these observations led Darwin to propose his theory of evolution by natural selection. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: During his voyage (1831-1836), Darwin observed fossils of extinct giant mammals in South America, such as glyptodonts (giant armadillo-like creatures) and giant ground sloths. He noticed that these fossils resembled living species found in the same regions (e.g., armadillos and sloths). This suggested that extinct species were related to modern species and that species change over time, rather than being fixed and unchanging since creation.

step_by_step.step: Observation 2: Finches of the Galapagos Islands (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: Darwin observed that finches on the Galapagos Islands varied in beak size and shape from island to island. Each species of finch had a beak adapted to a specific food source: large, strong beaks for cracking hard seeds; slender, pointed beaks for insects; and beak shapes suited for cactus flowers or other foods. He also noted that these finches were similar to mainland South American finches, suggesting they had colonized the islands and then diversified.

step_by_step.step: How observations led to natural selection theory (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Darwin synthesized his observations into a coherent theory: (1) From fossils, he concluded species change over time (descent with modification). (2) From finches, he inferred that species adapt to different environments. (3) Combining these with Malthus's ideas on population growth and his knowledge of artificial selection, he reasoned that individuals with advantageous heritable traits are more likely to survive and reproduce. Over generations, these traits accumulate, leading to adaptation and eventually new species.

problems.final_answer: (a) Observations: fossils of extinct giant mammals resembling living species in South America; Galapagos finches with different beak shapes adapted to different foods. (b) Darwin concluded that species change over time (descent with modification) and that natural selection—differential survival and reproduction of individuals with advantageous heritable traits—drives adaptation to local environments.

problems.problem: Distinguish between: (a) Directional selection (2 marks) (b) Stabilising selection (2 marks) (c) Give an example of when each type might occur. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Directional selection is a mode of natural selection in which an extreme phenotype is favored over other phenotypes, causing the population's trait distribution to shift in one direction over time. It occurs when environmental conditions change in a consistent direction, favoring individuals at one extreme of the phenotypic range. As a result, the mean phenotype of the population moves toward that extreme.

Stabilising selection is a mode of natural selection in which intermediate phenotypes are favored over extreme phenotypes. This reduces variation in the population and maintains the status quo, selecting against individuals at both extremes of the phenotypic range. Stabilising selection is the most common form of natural selection in stable environments.

Directional selection example: The peppered moth during the Industrial Revolution—dark moths were favored over light moths. Stabilising selection example: Human birth weight—very low and very high birth weight infants have lower survival rates than those of intermediate weight.

problems.final_answer: (a) Directional selection favors one extreme phenotype, shifting the population mean in that direction. (b) Stabilising selection favors intermediate phenotypes, reducing variation and maintaining the status quo. (c) Directional: industrial melanism in moths; stabilising: human birth weight.

problems.problem: (a) Explain how fossils provide evidence for evolution. (3 marks) (b) Suggest why the fossil record is incomplete. (2 marks)

step_by_step.step: Fossils as evidence for evolution (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: Fossils provide multiple lines of evidence: (1) Chronological succession: older rock layers contain simpler organisms, younger layers contain more complex forms. (2) Transitional forms: fossils like Tiktaalik and Archaeopteryx show links between major groups. (3) Extinct species: fossils document that many species no longer exist. (4) Biogeographic patterns: fossil distributions match current species distributions, supporting continental drift and common ancestry.

step_by_step.step: Why fossil record is incomplete (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: The fossil record is incomplete because: (1) Fossilization is rare, requiring rapid burial and hard parts. (2) Many fossils are destroyed by geological processes like erosion and subduction. (3) Only a fraction of fossil-bearing rock is exposed and explored. (4) There are gaps in sediment deposition (unconformities).

problems.final_answer: (a) Fossils show chronological change over time, provide transitional forms linking major groups, and document extinction of species. (b) The fossil record is incomplete because fossilization is rare (requires rapid burial and hard parts), many fossils are destroyed by geological processes, and only a fraction of rock layers have been explored.

problems.problem: (a) Distinguish between homologous structures and analogous structures. (2 marks) (b) Explain how homologous structures provide evidence for common ancestry. (2 marks) (c) Give one example of analogous structures. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Homologous structures share a common underlying structural plan and developmental origin, even if they serve different functions (e.g., mammal forelimbs). Analogous structures serve similar functions but have different structural origins and arise through convergent evolution (e.g., bird and insect wings).

step_by_step.step: Homologous structures as evidence for common ancestry (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Homologous structures indicate common ancestry because they are inherited from a shared ancestor and then modified for different functions. The same basic bone pattern in whale flippers, bat wings, and human arms is best explained by descent from a common ancestor with that limb structure, not by independent evolution.
Bird wings and insect wings are analogous structures—both enable flight but evolved independently from different ancestral structures.

problems.final_answer: (a) Homologous structures share common structure and origin (e.g., mammal forelimbs); analogous structures serve similar functions but have different origins (convergent evolution). (b) Homologous structures indicate common ancestry because they are inherited from a shared ancestor and then modified. (c) Example: bird wings and insect wings.

problems.problem: Describe how similarities in embryonic development between different vertebrates provide evidence for evolution.

step_by_step.explanation: Embryos of different vertebrates (fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals) show remarkable similarities in early development: all possess pharyngeal pouches (gill slits), a post-anal tail, and a similar basic body plan. Human embryos have pharyngeal arches and a tail that later regress.

These similarities are best explained by common ancestry. All vertebrates inherited the same basic developmental program from a shared ancestor. During evolution, different groups modified this program to produce their adult forms. The presence of ancestral features in embryos (like tails in human embryos) reflects this shared evolutionary history.

problems.final_answer: Vertebrate embryos show similar structures in early development—pharyngeal pouches, post-anal tails, and basic body plans. These similarities indicate common ancestry: all vertebrates inherited the same developmental program from a shared ancestor, which was later modified to produce different adult forms.

problems.problem: Explain how comparisons of DNA and protein sequences between species can be used to determine evolutionary relationships.

step_by_step.explanation: All organisms use DNA as genetic material. Mutations accumulate over time, so when two species diverge from a common ancestor, their DNA sequences gradually become more different. The more similar the sequences, the more recent the common ancestor.

Scientists compare specific genes (e.g., mitochondrial DNA, ribosomal RNA, cytochrome c) across species. Computer algorithms align sequences and calculate differences, which are used to construct phylogenetic trees showing evolutionary relationships.

Genes that mutate at a relatively constant rate can serve as a 'molecular clock' to estimate divergence times. This has revolutionized our understanding of relationships, sometimes revealing unexpected connections (e.g., whales are closely related to hippos).

problems.final_answer: DNA and protein sequences change over time as mutations accumulate. Species with more similar sequences share a more recent common ancestor. By comparing sequences of genes across species, scientists can construct phylogenetic trees showing evolutionary relationships and estimate divergence times using molecular clocks.

problems.problem: (a) Define artificial selection. (2 marks) (b) Describe how artificial selection differs from natural selection. (2 marks) (c) Give one example of artificial selection in agriculture or animal breeding. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Artificial selection (selective breeding) is the intentional process by which humans breed plants or animals with desirable traits to produce offspring that inherit those traits, increasing the frequency of desirable alleles over generations.

Artificial selection differs from natural selection: (1) Humans are the selective agent, not the environment. (2) It aims for traits beneficial to humans, not necessarily fitness in the wild. (3) It can produce rapid change over few generations. (4) It often reduces genetic diversity.
Modern dairy cows have been selectively bred for high milk production. Holstein cows produce over 10 times more milk than their ancestors.

problems.final_answer: (a) Artificial selection is human-directed breeding of organisms with desirable traits to produce offspring with those traits. (b) Natural selection is driven by environmental pressures; artificial selection is driven by human choice. (c) Example: selective breeding of dairy cows for high milk production.

problems.problem: Explain what is meant by 'genetic drift' and describe a situation where it is likely to have a significant effect on a population.

step_by_step.explanation: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequencies due to chance events, particularly significant in small populations. It can lead to loss or fixation of alleles purely by chance, not because they affect fitness.

Genetic drift operates through: (1) Founder effect: when a small group colonizes a new area, the new population's gene pool is a random sample. (2) Population bottleneck: when a population is drastically reduced, surviving individuals represent a random sample, reducing genetic diversity.

step_by_step.step: Situation where genetic drift is significant
step_by_step.explanation: Northern elephant seals were hunted to near-extinction (as few as 20 individuals) in the 19th century. The current population of over 100,000 has extremely low genetic diversity due to this bottleneck—a classic example of genetic drift.

problems.final_answer: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequencies due to chance events, especially significant in small populations. It occurs through founder effects or population bottlenecks. Example: northern elephant seals, reduced to ~20 individuals by hunting, now have very low genetic diversity.

problems.problem: Define gene flow and explain how it can affect the genetic diversity of populations.

step_by_step.explanation: Gene flow (migration) is the transfer of alleles from one population to another through movement of individuals and successful reproduction. It is one of the four main mechanisms of evolution.

step_by_step.step: Effect on genetic diversity: Increasing diversity
step_by_step.explanation: Gene flow increases genetic diversity within populations by introducing new alleles, counteracting genetic drift, reducing inbreeding, and providing adaptive potential. For example, gene flow between wild and cultivated crops can introduce disease resistance genes.

step_by_step.step: Effect on genetic diversity: Decreasing diversity (homogenization)
step_by_step.explanation: High gene flow reduces genetic differentiation between populations, making them more similar. This can limit local adaptation by swamping beneficial alleles with maladaptive ones. Between-population diversity decreases as populations homogenize.

problems.final_answer: Gene flow is the movement of alleles between populations through migration and interbreeding. It increases genetic diversity within populations by introducing new alleles, but can decrease diversity between populations by homogenizing them. High gene flow can prevent local adaptation and speciation.

problems.problem: Explain how allopatric speciation occurs. Include in your answer: (a) The initial event that isolates populations (2 marks) (b) How the populations become genetically different (2 marks) (c) Why they cannot interbreed if they meet again (2 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Allopatric speciation begins with a geographic barrier that physically separates a population, preventing gene flow. Barriers include mountain formation, continental drift, river formation, or colonization of remote islands. The separated populations evolve independently.

Isolated populations diverge through: (1) Natural selection in different environments. (2) Genetic drift (especially in small populations). (3) Independent mutations accumulating over time. These processes lead to differences in gene pools, morphology, and behavior.

If populations meet again, they cannot interbreed because they have evolved reproductive isolating mechanisms. Prezygotic barriers (different mating behaviors, breeding seasons) prevent mating; postzygotic barriers (hybrid inviability or sterility) prevent fertile offspring.

problems.final_answer: (a) Geographic isolation (e.g., mountain formation, island colonization) separates populations, preventing gene flow. (b) Separated populations diverge through natural selection, genetic drift, and independent mutations. (c) They cannot interbreed because they have evolved reproductive isolating mechanisms (prezygotic or postzygotic barriers).

problems.problem: Distinguish between allopatric and sympatric speciation. Give one example of how sympatric speciation might occur.

step_by_step.explanation: Allopatric speciation occurs when populations become geographically separated by a physical barrier, preventing gene flow and allowing divergence. This is the most common mode of speciation, especially in animals.

Sympatric speciation occurs without geographic separation. New species evolve within the same area through mechanisms like ecological specialization, sexual selection, or polyploidy (chromosome duplication, common in plants).

Polyploidy in plants: evening primrose (Oenothera gigas) arose from O. lamarckiana through chromosome doubling. Polyploid individuals are reproductively isolated from the parent species because triploid offspring are sterile.

problems.final_answer: Allopatric speciation involves geographic isolation; sympatric speciation occurs without geographic separation. Example: polyploidy in plants (chromosome doubling creates reproductive isolation from parent species).

problems.problem: Explain what is meant by 'adaptive radiation' and describe one example.

step_by_step.explanation: Adaptive radiation is the rapid diversification of a single ancestral species into multiple species that occupy different ecological niches. It occurs when a species colonizes a new environment with diverse opportunities or when a key innovation evolves.

Approximately 2-3 million years ago, a finch population colonized the Galapagos Islands. It diversified into about 15 species with different beak shapes adapted to different food sources: ground finches with strong beaks for seeds, tree finches for insects, warbler finches for probing, and cactus finches for cactus flowers.

problems.final_answer: Adaptive radiation is the rapid diversification of a single ancestral species into multiple species that occupy different ecological niches. Example: Darwin's finches—one finch species colonized the Galapagos Islands and diversified into ~15 species with different beak shapes adapted to different food sources.

problems.problem: (a) Define mass extinction. (2 marks) (b) Describe two causes of extinction. (2 marks) (c) Explain why the rate of extinction is currently higher than the background rate. (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: A mass extinction is the rapid loss of a large proportion (typically over 75%) of Earth's species in a short geological period. Five major mass extinctions are documented in the fossil record, with the End-Permian (The Great Dying) being the most severe.

Natural causes include: (1) Catastrophic events: meteorite impacts (e.g., K-Pg extinction), massive volcanic eruptions (e.g., Siberian Traps). (2) Habitat loss and climate change: glaciation, warming, sea-level changes. Human-caused extinctions result from habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, and invasive species.

Current extinction rates are estimated to be 100-1000 times higher than the natural background rate due to human activities: habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, climate change, and introduction of invasive species.

problems.final_answer: (a) Mass extinction is the rapid loss of a large proportion (over 75%) of Earth's species in a short geological period. (b) Causes: catastrophic events (meteorite impacts, volcanic eruptions) and habitat loss/climate change. (c) Current extinction rates are 100-1000 times higher than background due to human activities: habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, and climate change.

problems.problem: (a) Define biodiversity. (2 marks) (b) Describe three levels at which biodiversity can be measured. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Biodiversity is the variety of life on Earth at all levels of biological organization, including the diversity of species, genetic variation within and between species, and the diversity of ecosystems and ecological processes.

Genetic diversity is the variation in genes within and between populations of a species. High genetic diversity allows populations to adapt to changing environments. It can be measured by allele frequencies, heterozygosity, or polymorphism.

Species diversity has two components: species richness (number of species) and species evenness (relative abundance). It can be measured using indices like Shannon-Wiener or Simpson's diversity index.

Ecosystem diversity is the variety of ecosystems, habitats, and ecological processes within a region. It includes different ecosystem types (forests, wetlands, coral reefs) and the functions they perform.

problems.final_answer: (a) Biodiversity is the variety of life at all levels: genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity. (b) Three levels: genetic diversity (variation within species), species diversity (richness and evenness), and ecosystem diversity (variety of habitats and ecological processes).

problems.problem: Explain why biodiversity is important. Include reference to: (a) Ecosystem stability (2 marks) (b) Economic value (2 marks) (c) Ethical reasons (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Biodiversity enhances ecosystem stability: diverse communities are more productive, resistant to disturbances (insurance hypothesis), and resilient to change. Biodiversity underpins ecosystem services like pollination, nutrient cycling, water purification, and climate regulation.

Biodiversity has direct economic value (food, medicine, timber, ecotourism), indirect value (pollination estimated at $200-500 billion annually, water purification, flood control), and future option value (potential undiscovered resources). Over half of global GDP depends on nature.

Ethical reasons include: species have intrinsic value regardless of human use; humans have a moral obligation as stewards of the planet to preserve biodiversity for future generations (intergenerational equity); many cultures have spiritual connections to nature.

problems.final_answer: (a) Biodiversity enhances ecosystem stability—diverse ecosystems are more productive, resistant to disturbances, and resilient to change. (b) Economic value: direct use (food, medicine, timber), indirect use (pollination, water purification, climate regulation), and future option value. (c) Ethical reasons: species have intrinsic value, and humans have a moral responsibility to preserve biodiversity for future generations.

problems.problem: (a) Describe two causes of habitat destruction. (2 marks) (b) Explain how habitat destruction leads to loss of biodiversity. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Deforestation is the clearing of forests for agriculture (cattle ranching, palm oil, soy), logging, and infrastructure development. Tropical forests have lost about 17% of their area in the past 50 years.

step_by_step.step: Cause 2: Urbanization and land conversion (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: Urbanization converts natural habitats to cities, roads, and industrial areas. Agricultural intensification transforms diverse habitats into monocultures. Coastal development destroys mangroves, coral reefs, and wetlands.

step_by_step.step: How habitat destruction reduces biodiversity (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Habitat destruction reduces biodiversity through: (1) Direct loss of species when habitats are cleared. (2) Habitat fragmentation isolates populations, reducing gene flow and increasing extinction risk. (3) Disruption of ecological interactions (pollination, seed dispersal, food webs). (4) Extinction debt—delayed extinctions after habitat loss. (5) Cascade effects when keystone species are lost.

problems.final_answer: (a) Causes: deforestation (clearing forests for agriculture, logging, or development) and urbanization (converting natural habitats to cities, roads, and farmland). (b) Habitat destruction reduces biodiversity by directly killing species, fragmenting remaining habitats (isolating populations), disrupting ecological interactions, and making species more vulnerable to extinction.

problems.problem: Describe how pollution can reduce biodiversity. Give one specific example in your answer.

step_by_step.step: Mechanisms of pollution impacts on biodiversity
step_by_step.explanation: Pollution reduces biodiversity through: direct toxicity killing sensitive species; habitat degradation; bioaccumulation and biomagnification in food chains; disruption of ecosystem processes; reproductive impairment (endocrine disruptors); and eutrophication causing dead zones.

step_by_step.step: Specific example: Eutrophication in the Gulf of Mexico
step_by_step.explanation: Agricultural fertilizers from the Mississippi River cause algal blooms. When algae die, bacterial decomposition consumes oxygen, creating a hypoxic 'dead zone' up to 20,000 km². Fish, shrimp, and bottom-dwelling organisms cannot survive, reducing biodiversity and harming commercial fisheries.

problems.final_answer: Pollution reduces biodiversity through direct toxicity, habitat degradation, eutrophication (nutrient pollution causing dead zones), and bioaccumulation of toxins. Example: Agricultural fertilizer runoff creates dead zones in the Gulf of Mexico—excess nutrients cause algal blooms that decompose, depleting oxygen and killing fish and bottom-dwelling organisms.

problems.problem: (a) Define invasive species. (2 marks) (b) Explain how invasive species can threaten native biodiversity. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: An invasive species is a non-native organism that establishes in a new environment and causes harm to native ecosystems, human health, or the economy. Not all introduced species become invasive; they must establish, spread, and cause negative impacts.

Invasive species outcompete natives for resources. They often have advantages: no natural predators, faster growth, higher reproduction, or allelopathic chemicals. Zebra mussels invaded the Great Lakes, outcompeting native mussels and altering ecosystems.

Invasive predators devastate naive native species that lack defenses. Brown tree snakes introduced to Guam caused extinction of 12 bird species. Rats on islands prey on bird eggs and chicks, reducing seabird populations by over 90% on many Pacific islands.

step_by_step.step: Mechanism 3: Habitat modification and disease (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Invasive species alter habitats (water hyacinth forms dense mats, blocking light and oxygen) and introduce novel diseases. The chytrid fungus, spread globally, has caused extinction of over 200 amphibian species.

problems.final_answer: (a) An invasive species is a non-native organism that establishes in a new environment and causes harm to native ecosystems, economy, or human health. (b) Invasive species threaten native biodiversity by: outcompeting natives for resources; preying on naive native species; altering habitats; and introducing novel diseases.

problems.problem: Explain how climate change affects biodiversity. Include reference to: (a) Changes in distribution of species (2 marks) (b) Changes in phenology (timing of life cycles) (2 marks) (c) Increased extinction risk (1 mark)

step_by_step.explanation: Species shift ranges toward poles (average 17 km per decade for terrestrial, 72 km for marine) and to higher elevations to track suitable climates. Species with limited dispersal or barriers (islands, mountain summits) face range contractions.

Phenology shifts: earlier spring events (leaf-out, flowering, migration, breeding) create mismatches. For example, great tits in the Netherlands now breed later than the caterpillar peak they feed on, reducing chick survival.

Extinction risk increases as species cannot adapt or disperse fast enough. At 1.5°C warming, 6% of insects, 8% of plants, and 4% of vertebrates lose over half their range; at 3°C, these figures rise to 49%, 44%, and 26% respectively.

problems.final_answer: (a) Species shift ranges toward poles and higher elevations to track suitable climates. (b) Phenological changes: earlier spring events cause mismatches between interdependent species (e.g., birds and insect food peaks). (c) Extinction risk increases as species cannot adapt or disperse fast enough, especially those with limited ranges, specialized habitats, or barriers to movement.

problems.problem: Describe three methods used to conserve biodiversity. For each method, give a specific example.

step_by_step.explanation: Protected areas like national parks and nature reserves conserve species in their natural habitats, preserving ecological processes and interactions. Example: Yellowstone to Yukon Conservation Initiative connects protected areas across the Rocky Mountains, creating corridors for grizzly bears, wolves, and elk.

Captive breeding maintains endangered species in controlled environments for reintroduction when threats are reduced. Example: California condor was reduced to 27 individuals; captive breeding increased the population to over 500, with more than 300 now in the wild.

Seed banks store genetic material under controlled conditions as a safety net against extinction. Example: Svalbard Global Seed Vault stores over 1.2 million seed samples from nearly every country, serving as a backup for national seed banks.

problems.final_answer: (1) Protected areas (in situ): Yellowstone to Yukon Initiative—a network of connected reserves protecting grizzly bear populations. (2) Captive breeding and reintroduction: California condor—captive breeding from 27 individuals restored population to over 500. (3) Seed banks (ex situ): Svalbard Global Seed Vault—stores over 1.2 million seed samples as a backup against extinction and disaster.

problems.problem: (a) Explain the purpose of national parks and nature reserves. (2 marks) (b) Suggest one advantage and one disadvantage of protecting biodiversity in this way. (2 marks)

step_by_step.step: Purpose of national parks and nature reserves (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: National parks and nature reserves protect species in their natural habitats (in situ conservation), preserve ecosystems and ecological processes, maintain ecosystem services (clean water, carbon storage), provide refuges for biodiversity, and support research, education, and recreation.

Advantage: Protected areas conserve entire ecosystems and the complex interactions within them, protecting not only charismatic species but also the countless invertebrates, fungi, and microorganisms that constitute most biodiversity.

Disadvantage: Protected areas can displace local communities or restrict traditional resource use if established without adequate consultation. Historically, some national parks were created by forcibly removing indigenous peoples who had sustainably managed these lands.

problems.final_answer: (a) National parks and nature reserves protect species in their natural habitats (in situ conservation), preserve ecosystems and ecological processes, provide refuges for biodiversity, and maintain ecosystem services. (b) Advantage: protects entire ecosystems and the interactions within them. Disadvantage: can displace local communities or restrict traditional resource use if not managed with community participation.

problems.problem: (a) Describe the process of captive breeding. (2 marks) (b) Give two advantages and two disadvantages of captive breeding programmes. (3 marks)

step_by_step.explanation: Captive breeding involves: establishing a founder population; genetic management using studbooks to avoid inbreeding; providing appropriate husbandry; managing population size; and eventually reintroducing individuals to the wild when threats are addressed.

Advantages: (1) Prevents extinction when wild populations are critically low (e.g., California condor). (2) Enables reintroduction to restore populations in their historical range (e.g., black-footed ferret, Arabian oryx).

Disadvantages: (1) Loss of genetic diversity over time due to small founder numbers and genetic drift. (2) Behavioral changes: captive animals may lose natural behaviors (predator recognition, foraging skills) needed for survival in the wild.

problems.final_answer: (a) Captive breeding involves maintaining and breeding endangered species in controlled environments with careful genetic management (studbooks) to maintain diversity. (b) Advantages: prevents extinction (e.g., California condor) and enables reintroduction to the wild. Disadvantages: loss of genetic diversity over time and behavioral changes that reduce survival in the wild.

problems.problem: Explain the purpose of seed banks and describe how they help to conserve biodiversity.

step_by_step.explanation: Seed banks store seeds under controlled conditions (low temperature, low humidity) to preserve genetic diversity for decades or centuries. They serve as a safety net against extinction, protect crop wild relatives for food security, and provide material for restoration and research.

Seed banks conserve biodiversity by: (1) Long-term preservation of genetic material. (2) Systematic collection of seeds representing genetic diversity across species' ranges. (3) Providing material for habitat restoration and reintroduction. (4) Conserving crop diversity essential for breeding climate-resilient varieties. (5) Enabling research on plant genetics and climate adaptation.

problems.final_answer: Seed banks store seeds of plant species under controlled conditions to preserve genetic diversity for long periods. They conserve biodiversity by: protecting against extinction (safety net), providing material for habitat restoration, conserving crop wild relatives for food security, and enabling research and climate adaptation. Example: Svalbard Global Seed Vault stores over 1.2 million seed samples as a global backup.

problems.problem: Explain what is meant by 'sustainable development' and describe how it can help to maintain biodiversity while meeting human needs.

step_by_step.explanation: Sustainable development meets present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs. It balances economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection, recognizing that human well-being depends on healthy ecosystems.

step_by_step.step: How sustainable development helps maintain biodiversity
step_by_step.explanation: Sustainable development maintains biodiversity by: establishing protected areas with community benefits (e.g., ecotourism); promoting sustainable resource use (FSC forestry, MSC fisheries); supporting sustainable agriculture (agroecology, shade-grown coffee); integrating green infrastructure in cities; addressing climate change; and transitioning to a circular economy.

step_by_step.step: Examples of sustainable development in practice
step_by_step.explanation: Example: Namibia's communal conservancies give local communities rights to manage and benefit from wildlife. Communities earn income from sustainable trophy hunting and ecotourism, leading to increases in elephant, rhino, and lion populations while providing jobs and income.

problems.final_answer: Sustainable development meets present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs. It helps maintain biodiversity by: integrating conservation into economic planning (e.g., ecotourism, sustainable forestry, certified fisheries); creating protected areas with community benefits; promoting sustainable agriculture; and addressing climate change—allowing human development while preserving ecosystems and species.

problems.problem: A small population of rare birds lives on an isolated island. A new species of predatory rat is accidentally introduced to the island. (a) Predict what is likely to happen to the bird population and explain why. (3 marks) (b) Suggest three conservation measures that could be taken to protect the bird population. (3 marks)

step_by_step.step: Prediction: Population decline and possible extinction (part a)
step_by_step.explanation: The bird population will likely decline sharply and may become extinct because: (1) Island birds evolved without mammalian predators and lack defenses. (2) Rats prey on eggs, chicks, and adults. (3) The small population size makes it vulnerable to any increase in mortality. (4) Rats can access nests in trees and on the ground.

step_by_step.step: Conservation measure 1: Eradication of the rats (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Eradicate the rat population using aerial or ground-based rodenticides. This has been successful on hundreds of islands (e.g., South Georgia Island), allowing seabird populations to recover. Biosecurity measures must prevent re-invasion.

step_by_step.step: Conservation measure 2: Translocation to predator-free habitat (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Translocate birds to a predator-free island or secure enclosure. New Zealand has successfully moved endangered species like kākāpō to predator-free offshore islands, increasing populations from near-extinction to sustainable numbers.

step_by_step.step: Conservation measure 3: Intensive population management (part b)
step_by_step.explanation: Immediate measures: install rat-proof nest boxes; implement sustained trapping around breeding areas; provide supplementary feeding; conduct intensive population monitoring; and establish a captive breeding population off-island as a last resort.

problems.final_answer: (a) The bird population will likely decline sharply and may become extinct because island birds often lack defenses against mammalian predators; rats will prey on eggs, chicks, and adults, and the small population size makes it vulnerable. (b) Conservation measures: (1) Eradicate rats using rodenticides; (2) Translocate birds to a predator-free island; (3) Protect nests with rat-proof barriers and implement intensive trapping around breeding sites.
